comments:
Summary:
This paper evaluates Deep Belief Networks (DBNs) applied to high-resolution, multichannel EEG data for seizure detection. The authors use a 9-feature-per-channel featurization (23 channels → 207 features) adapted from prior work, compare several classifiers (k-NN, SVM, logistic regression) and a DBN + logistic-regression top layer, and report F1-based results for within-patient and leave-one-patient-out experiments. The paper also analyzes memory and computation costs with an eye toward embedded deployment.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a practical problem (seizure detection) on clinical-quality multichannel data and considers runtime/storage constraints relevant to ambulatory/embedded devices.
- The experimental design includes both within-patient and across-patient (leave-one-out) conditions, which is important to evaluate generalization.
- The authors provide implementation and training details (DBN topology, learning rates, epochs) and a clear description of the features used, along with a quantitative complexity/memory analysis.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Novelty is limited. The feature set and dataset are taken from prior work and the DBN is applied to hand-crafted features rather than directly to raw signals. This reduces the novelty relative to other deep-learning work that attempts end-to-end learning from raw time series.
- The evaluation lacks important details and statistical rigor: exact dataset sizes (total seconds per patient), number of seizure vs non-seizure samples per split, and confidence intervals or statistical significance tests are not reported. Given strong class imbalance (85–99% non-seizure), more care is needed in reporting and interpreting results.
- Comparisons to prior published results on the same dataset (e.g., Shoeb & Guttag) are only discussed qualitatively; no quantitative side-by-side comparison of performance metrics is provided. This makes it hard to judge whether the proposed approach advances the state of the art.
- The DBN is trained on precomputed features; the rationale for not attempting representation learning from raw EEG (or at least comparing both approaches) is not fully argued. Many modern deep approaches learn better features end-to-end.
- Complexity analysis is useful but remains mostly analytic/relative; actual runtime, memory footprint, and energy measurements on representative embedded hardware (or at least model sizes and inference latency numbers) would strengthen the claims about suitability for low-power devices.
- Hyperparameter selection and validation procedures are underspecified (trial-and-error mentioned; no grid search details or validation curves). Overfitting risk is not discussed in detail.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide more complete dataset statistics (per-patient counts of seizure/non-seizure seconds), and report per-experiment confusion matrices, ROC/AUC, and confidence intervals or significance tests for comparisons.
- Include a direct quantitative comparison to prior methods on the same dataset (report their numbers and compare), or, if unavailable, run baseline reimplementations to contextualize performance.
- Consider experiments that train models end-to-end on raw EEG (or compare featurized vs raw inputs) to justify the use of DBNs on precomputed features.
- Report absolute model sizes (MB), inference latency, and (ideally) measured energy usage or simulated estimates for embedded deployment to substantiate the complexity claims.
- Clarify hyperparameter tuning procedures and add ablation studies (e.g., number of layers/nodes, effect of truncation/normalization thresholds, impact of each feature class).

Recommendation rationale:
The paper presents a practical experimental study that will be of interest to researchers working on EEG-based seizure detection and embedded health sensing. However, the technical contribution is incremental, and the evaluation needs to be more rigorous and better contextualized with related published results. The complexity analysis is a valuable contribution but would be stronger with measured device-level results. With revisions addressing the evaluation and comparisons, the paper would be suitable for publication in a workshop or conference poster session.
